result:
I keep a shoebox of obsolete things on the highest shelf of the closet I never open: a Nokia with a cracked faceplate that once stored six text messages from a boy who smelled like chlorine; a folded paper map of a state I drove through once at nineteen, the route traced in felt-tip ink now bled into fuzzy pilgrimage; a single VHS tape labeled, in my father’s tidy engineer’s script, “1998—Fireworks & Karen’s Wedding,” though I have no machine left to confirm the footage even exists.  

The box is my private museum of entropy, proof that I was alive in formats you can no longer play.  

Sometimes, late at night, when the apartment ticks like cooling metal and the streetlights sodium-stain the blinds, I climb for the box and listen to the objects rustle like small animals waking. They know more about me than cloud storage ever will; they remember the exact tremble in my thumb as I deleted that boy’s last message so my mother wouldn’t find it, the static electricity that lifted the map’s edge when I veered too fast toward the shoulder, sobbing along with a mixtape that unspooled itself into the passenger footwell.  

Digital ghosts don’t haunt like analog ones. A corrupted file presents an abrupt coffin-lid; a tape unravels gradually, ribboning the air with mournful applause.  

I think we misunderstand time. We picture it as a river, but it is more like confetti shot from a cannon on a windy day: thousands of bright scraps descending at different velocities, occasionally lifting again to stick against windows, hair, wet pavement. The past is not upstream; it flutters beside you, sometimes adhering to your skin so firmly you mistake it for the present. Other times it drifts off and you find it years later in the lint screen of a laundromat dryer, color bled but still recognizable.  

Last week a physicist on the radio said that information is never truly destroyed, only scrambled. Give him enough chalkboard and he’ll recover the smell of your grandmother’s kitchen from the faint infrared echo still ricocheting against the expanding edge of the universe. I almost called the station to ask if he could locate the exact moment my father stopped believing in the future, but I feared he’d reply that the data exists yet is encrypted by shame.  

Instead, I went to the river—not the metaphorical one—and skimmed flat stones across its skin. Each skip felt like trying to resend a text message to the dead: the ripples spread, received by no device but the muddy banks. On the eighth throw the stone vanished mid-arc, swallowed by a gust of wind that sounded uncannily like applause from a VHS tape.  

I came home and googled “how to digitize VHS without a VCR,” which is the twenty-first-century equivalent of “how to resurrect a person without a body.” The internet offered contraptions that resemble miniature garbage trucks for memories: feed in the spools, let USB ingest what magnetic tape can no longer confess. But the reviews complained of dropped frames, color bands, audio drift—symptoms identical to dementia.  

I closed the tab and stared instead at the empty rectangle on the wall where the Nokia’s charger once hung like a snake’s shed skin. The outlet beneath it still bears a tiny scorch mark from the night lightning struck the building while I was telling the chlorine boy I loved him for the first time. The text went through anyway, the network routing our pulses around thunderheads. He replied with a semicolon and closed parenthesis, emoji before emoji, which I decoded as a promise even though it looked like half a wink.  

When people leave, they rarely take their fingerprints off the things you can’t archive: the indentation in the carpet where his duffel bag waited, the ghosted breath on the window when he drew a heart, then wiped it clean. My phone now holds 128 gigabytes, none of which can store the weight of a duffel bag.  

I read once that astronauts on the International Space Station grow up to three percent taller in microgravity, their spines decompressing like unzippered luggage. Upon return to Earth they shrink again, but the extra centimeters are never completely regained; somewhere in the vertebrae remains a quiet spaciousness originally intended for stars. I wonder what equivalent elongation happens to the heart after it spends extended periods outside gravity’s surveillance—say, an entire adolescence, or the first year after a parent dies.  

There should be a medical term for the residual space, a diagnosis code to bill the cosmos for the difference.  

Instead, we keep boxes.  

Tonight the box hums louder than usual, so I set it on the floor and remove the lid like a bomb technician who has forgotten which wire is memory. The Nokia’s screen stays dark, but when I tilt it the cracked glass refracts the ceiling bulb into a constellation identical to the one we watched through the sunroof of a borrowed sedan the night Karen got married. The wedding was in July; the fireworks were for the Fourth, borrowed from a town that believed patriotism and matrimony shared color palettes. My father held the camcorder on his shoulder like a bazooka, narrating the scene with understated Scandinavian awe: “Would you look at that, you can almost see the curvature.”  

Karen divorced six years later, but the tape—if it still magnetizes—preserves the curvature he imagined, a horizon where love and country both exploded brilliantly before dissipating into smoke you could taste in your hair for days.  

I slot the phone into my palm, press the power button though I know the battery is lithium confetti somewhere in a landfill. Still, the muscle memory surprises me: thumb circling the four-directional keypad, phantom limb of longing. I half-expect the screen to bloom green with a message that traveled fourteen years through dead satellites to tell me the boy finally learned how to pronounce his own fear.  

Instead, the apartment buzzer goes off: a courier downstairs with a parcel containing a refurbished VCR I do not remember ordering. Maybe entropy has an Amazon account. I buzz him up because I am polite, even to algorithms.  

While I wait, I unfold the paper map. Along the bleeding ink I once wrote EXIT 187—THE PLACE WE ALMOST DIED, though the interstate only numbers up to 112 in that state. Cartographers call such errors “paper towns,” invented hamlets that function as copyright traps. I suppose lovers invent them too, mythical locations where the relationship almost survived.  

The courier knocks. He is younger than the Nokia, wears a uniform the color of unresolved sky. He hands me the box sans eye contact, already moving toward the elevator, because time is routing instructions and he has infinite next stops.  

Back inside, I slit cardboard. The VCR exhales dust like an elderly dragon woken from irrelevant hibernation. Its LED display bleats 12:00, 12:00, 12:00—Morse code for eternity or maybe just lunch. I consider inserting Karen’s tape, but hesitate: if the footage refuses to play, does the wedding still qualify as having happened? Philosophers of media call this “format oblivion,” but I call it Sunday.  

Instead, I place the Nokia on top of the VCR like a tiny monarch. Two obsolete vessels courting one another across the gulf of protocol mismatch. Perhaps they’ll negotiate a truce: analog longing trading itself for digital refusal until a new frequency emerges—one that carries the scent of chlorine, the hush of highway shoulders, the precise way my father whispered curvature like a prayer.  

I allow myself ten minutes of anticipation. Nothing happens, naturally. Entropy is not a miracle worker; it’s middle management for decay.  

Still, when I close the closet door, I swear the shoebox feels lighter, as if some fragment finally transferred itself into the room’s thin air. Maybe tomorrow my thumb will brush the scorched outlet and remember lightning as approval, or the window will collect a breath-heart that nobody draws, only witnesses.  

Outside, dawn smears itself across the brickwork like corrupted pixels, but beautiful anyway. The river of confetti keeps falling, and somewhere in the expanding infrared, my grandmother’s kitchen, my father’s forgotten faith, and the semicolon wink all travel together at the speed of last chances.  

I have 1000 words; the word after this one will be silence, which is itself just another format, undestroyed, waiting to be replayed.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
